#blacklivesmatter here's a little guide on creating auto-populated email templates & make it easy for people to email reps with demands!

1) Go to a Mail-to generator eg. https://t.co/iPBrDVBDKd and make the template

2) Shorten the link at https://t.co/Uqcgs8zt1e and share it!

If you launch this link on a phone it opens the email app with the message and recipients populated.

Also, make sure you launch and preview the message before sharing! (eg. occasionally apostrophes do odd things, so you can just go back and write that contraction out)

Emails to reps should always have a space for the sender's name and city/state/zip, and a signature line.

And lastly, for anyone: ALWAYS read a template entirely before sending...to make sure there's no garbage in there, and you stand behind everything it says :)

Friendly reminder to not assume what people are/are not doing! Some super active people I know don't even use their social media often. Posting can be good for sharing info but it isn't a substitute for direct action. The most important thing is to focus on your own contribution.

& also, it's unfair to assume that reposting information is just "performative." (Performative = Posting info without doing anything, and you wouldn't know what people really do.) If you've seen a repost & dialed a number, signed, or donated as a result, sharing info is working.

Why do police resort to lethal force so often?? I'm thinking about Ma'Khia Bryant and Daunte Wright, and I always think back to Scout Shultz back in 2017 because it happened when I was a student at Georgia Tech. Nothing warrants this kind of escalation.
https://t.co/AvR2fR29Ht

The whole system is the problem: Many of the people in it, the training (scapegoating to say "lack of training" in the case of Scout), subjective application of "protocol" resulting in unwarranted escalation, little to no consequences for cops that murder. And it never ENDS.

This system is archaic, unsustainable, and unreformable. Efforts need to go towards creating an entirely new system with a culture that does not enable escalation, violence, and brutality (& don't get me started on racial disparities) that we see from the current policing system.

When we say it's unreformable we say that because the problems with the entire system are so deep and widespread that reform is impossible at this point.

When we say abolish the policing system, we don't mean chaos and lack of safety. We mean resources for systemically (c)

disadvantaged communities (funding away from police and into housing, education, jobs, public services) to reduce crime overall.

We mean an alternative to the current, system that still ensures public safety & doesn't escalate in violence as the current policing system does (c)

and is focused on public safety with an understanding of mental health, racial biases (implicit and explicit), and restorative justice--and operates on a system of accountability (eg. abusive actors facing consequences). #AbolishThePolice #BLM #MakiahBryant #DaunteWright

Breaks my heart to see people that were once humble turn condescending and alienate others who don't think like them. That's not grounds for education, cooperation, or positive change. "Wow I can't believe how dumb [x group]/society/people are" <<< Staying optimistic and humble

What is the intent of condescension? What does it accomplish? Is it to blow hot air while standing on your soapbox, or is it to educate? And if the intent is truly to educate or spread awareness for change, why have you decided that berating groups is the way to do it?
This serves to only create a packing party of people who already agree with you while pushing away and "othering" the people who do not.

Movements need to be mindful of their messaging. Social media is a powerful distribution channel, and we have to ensure the quality of the distributed material. Posts focusing on vitriol and platitudes are missed opportunities for quality, educational material and action items.

On that note, know your audience.

Vitriol and platitudes: Appeal to only people who already agree with you. It's a draw, but it doesn't necessarily effect change alone.

Education: When it's fact-based and free of vitriol (do NOT alienate your audience) it provides information and space for others to come to their own conclusions. This has appeal to both those who do and do not agree with you, and especially to those who are capable of learning.

Action items: These have the most appeal to those who already agree with you. It's inefficient to try to convince those who do not--that's what education is for. People are the most engaged only if they've come to their own conclusions.
Education + action is how you get engagement that results in change. You can't have one without the other.

Personal effective messaging notes: Say what you mean and mean what you say.

- Don't say "things" because...what does that mean? Find the actual noun.

- Do not use "but" as it weakens the antecedent clause. Instead, try "and" and if "and" fails, you're not saying it effectively

- Do not make unsubstantiated claims that you can't back up with fact. Don't parrot what you read on social media without doing your own research first

- Where you mean to call out an individual bad actor, do exactly that. Avoid using broad labels/demographics

- Critical analysis followed by concrete solutions or a discussion on effective solutions makes "bad news" more palatable. Cynicism might feed emotional addiction to negative news but it's not conducive to motivation for positive, effective action.

- End on high notes. If you're going to bring them down (eg. painting the reality of a problem) bring them back up (eg. "here's how we're starting to see positive change" and "here's what you can do")

- Do not guilt trip or shame your audience. Educate. Use data where possible.

Clarification: This tweet should NOT be misconstrued to excuse those who say "not all men" or "not all cops" because that is derailment from the discussion of widespread problems. Rather, I mean "policing as system is broken" is more informative and educational than "ACAB"

In this case, the bad actor is "the system of policing" so your friend whose friend/dad/aunt is a cop won't take it personally, check out of the discussion, and disengage because they think you're attacking them. See what I mean?

In my experience the only people who understand what "ACAB" actually means are the ones that already agree with it when they hear it, have already been educated on it (by themselves or others), or identify with a partisan viewpoint so much that they blindly adopt it.
Vitriolic slogans such as this fail to educate those who may have understood a more nuanced explanation.

"The policing system has enabled brutality, murder, and lack of accountability, and corrupt relationships between police unions & politicians. It hasn't & won't change. Public safety warrants a new system, a new selection of people, new rules, & a new focus"...more informative

Fact-based education = data-driven. By which metric is this "better" policy? What are the health and/or cost savings? What's the data or science behind it--historically, or on a smaller implementation scale?

Also, sources. Is the data coming from a biased or objective source?

We should have an open-source educational system with standardized metrics of mastery, and it should be free to access and demonstrate. The current system of higher education favors those who can pay for a piece of paper (a diploma) and it's entirely inequitable.
And even then, when you have a diploma, that doesn't demonstrate mastery. We favor labels, grades, diplomas, degrees, and titles over learning and demonstrable skill.

ETS makes standardized tests. You can "master" them if your parents are rich and have expensive tutors for you, or you buy a lot of expensive prep books. You need to pay to take them, pay if you want to see detailed score reports, pay to retake. $$$ Why is THAT our standard?
Make access to quality internet and quality education, and demonstration of mastery of that education, a human right. Make it free.
The joys of deleting threaded tweets and redoing them bc you just hate the typos you made

Sometimes I think political labels do more harm than good. Someone that agrees with the logic and value of a proposal might shut down when they hear "That's what socialists say." And statements like "Fuck capitalism" are truly unproductive.

The US (like most developed countries) incorporates both socialist and capitalist practices. Capitalism isn't going anywhere and capitalist and socialist practices need each other to survive. We have to be realistic in our approach or we fail to have productive discussions.

I know people who receive progressive views well, but didn't vote @BernieSanders because the "socialist" part of his "Democratic socialist" label turned them off. & I know others that were galvanized by it to believe in equitable ideas, so due credit to the Overton window shift.

The "Progressive capitalist" label that @RoKhanna utilizes is strategically clever. So is using logical reasoning that appeals to your textbook capitalist eg. beyond ethos, arguments around lessening economic burdens and increasing productivity to justify "Progressive" policies.

Even typing these tweets, I'm having to use labels to concisely get across what I mean. Eg. "Progressive policies" I realized the irony. Free of labels, however, if the values, arguments, and policies in proposals are valid & truly in the public's best interest then use logic.

We need to be moving away from zero-sum, partisan rhetoric and into the territory of "win-win" in our appeals eg. Achieving the widest spread of dignity and prosperity: measured by equitable access to resources and equitable opportunity and consequential, projected outcomes.

And I feel those that speak in terms of the ethos and pathos on "the far left" *cringe* when I claim that sometimes to bridge the gap, you need to make arguments about how better health leads to better productivity leads to healthy workforce leads to more money.

It sounds hella capitalist because it's so focused on profit. And the reality IS that some people value that. You don't have to be in love with everyone's values or how they think. You DO, however, have to be able to get consensus and support if you want ideas to become reality.

I remember asking "Why do chameleons change color?" as a kid and hearing "So they can hide from predators" and feeling so frustrated because I meant "How" and wanted to know the science behind it. I only learned the explanations of nerves and chromatophores way later in life.

By then, however, I had diminished curiosity and usually accepted simple answers without the desire for mechanistic explanations that I had as a kid.

Kids are endlessly curious sponges for information, and the way questions are answered informs curiousity later in life.
I wonder how we improve the educational system to leverage childhood curiosity. Not everyone has access to science books, good teachers, or caregivers that provide them with answers and attention.

Personal observations on digital political discourse --

Memes and infographics: Used for getting ideas to gain traction and go viral. Eye-catching, often incorporate humor, easily shareable.

Drawback: Risk of easily spread reductionism & misinformation, sometimes redundant

Labels and slogans: Used for shifts in the Overton window. Catchy, people who already lean one way may be inclined to lean further. For people who already agree w you

Drawbacks: May be polarizing, groupthink, stereotype & misunderstanding prone, too high-level to be informative

Educational info eg. simple, mechanistic explanations with data: Explains a lot. Can share to educate people and they would draw their own conclusions

Drawbacks: The wrong tone (ANY aggression), obvious bias, cherry-picked data, annecdotal stories...these can be discrediting

It's arguably worse if someone has constructive feedback to offer, and holds back.

Reasons:
1) They don't believe it's within your comprehension - q of intelligence
2) They don't expect better from you - lack of investment or belief in you

Constructive criticism = respect 👍

Personal reflections:

1) I think about how little I think about pictures that all start to look so similar (food, travel)...I spend a single second on viewing individual pieces of lifestyle content. Therefore why should I believe that people care about anything similar I post?

2) There are definitely NOT 236 people on this planet (# of followers) who actually care what I'm up to, or know me. Maybe like...10. With 5 who actually care that I have on IG. The real ones are the ones I text and see irl and that's unchanging.

3) IG is a way for people to judge me based on appearance, "glow-ups," travel, style, whatever...what does that say about the individual I am? What does that say about how I think? Evaluate others and me off a face and a caption that say nothing about us as a person.
4) I've never felt like pictures do an experience justice. I've never felt like "doing it for the gram;" it detracts from my personal presence.

5) People spend a second tapping to like and move on, not thinking twice. Those numbers don't mean anything.

And the same goes for Twitter. More than anything social media is an outlet for expression for me, without expectation for feedback. Most days I'm talking to myself 🤷🏾‍♀️

In situations where emotions are running high for one or more parties, no amount of logic or objective reasoning will matter. Emotional validation, acknowledgment, and empathy can pave a path to understanding and common ground. Or, just pausing and reconvening when emotions calm.

Once on either common ground or *calmer* ground, curiosity and invitation to collaborate are crucial. Collaborative spaces are created when parties have the safety to share their thoughts and experiences. This requires treating and speaking to others with respect/like an equal.

In order to treat someone like an equal, one must believe "In this moment, I have just as much to learn from them as they do from me." Even if you disagree, you can still learn a lot when someone is able to freely express why they have the views they do.

Empathize and emotionally validate to de-escalate.

Emotionally validate and invite collaboration to create emotional safety and free expression.

Sustain collaboration and safety of expression by treating others like equals, with respect and empathy.

We fear AI because we are so overstimulated as a society that we commodify everything, forget how to think for ourselves, and evade connecting with immutable reality.

Entertainment? Streaming shows and music so we're never alone with our thoughts. Endless scrolling on social media for edited images in curated feeds by an algorithm to kill time and often to, again, escape our own thoughts.

And humans? When we swipe away on dating apps (we think) we have infinite options for connection. Commodity. Information spread? Easy. Repeat a trending opinion, often without doing reading. Everything is plenty, accessible, replaceable, keep it coming. That's how we often live.

And we worry that the cold artificiality of intelligence will replace all that we "care" about in our endless consumption, and how we think, but we already operate in the realm of distracted artifice. We forget how to see or care about anything real at all.

The mitigation: Slow down, be present, cut stimulation, and remember again how to think independently and critically. It takes conscious commitment to break from constant consumption. Learn how to be with your own thoughts, or to even have your own thoughts again. Stop running.

Understanding a culture with a different language can be obscured and limited by broad categorical vocabulary in the language of your own culture.
Eg. "Hindu" and "Hinduism" being a broad term coined by the Greek and Persians, originally tied to geography but then used to lump diverse, local spirituality and philosophy into one term.

Pay attention to when terms aren't etymologically coined by the people they describe and you uncover political strategy and motive. Find the actual terms used, historically, and it's a gold mine of beautiful yet often buried nuances
MO: Raising a curiosity flag when the term your language uses wasn't historically used or *isn't currently* used by the people it's describing

@danfaggella @astridwilde1 Personal example: Grew up w/ Bangladeshi Muslim parents and bc of their polarized Muslim/Hindu colonial era-affected views I didn't explore Hinduism until I learned about Buddhism, explored Eastern spirituality in general, and found spiritual alignment with Advaita Vedanta

@danfaggella @astridwilde1 For context, Advaita Vedanta is technically "Hinduism" but "Hindu" is a nondescript term in the sense that it can mean anything from monotheism to polytheism and believing in personal god(s) to not believing in any personal gods at all but just in universal consciousness/oneness

This body is a temple of the soul, currents of air flowing with every breath through corridors with nothing else inside it. Sometimes there's candlelight in the windows when eyes light up in moments of love. Moments.

Other times, in meditation, the current of the soul does not touch the walls and wonders what the space between itself and the wall is. It wonders what "I" and "you" mean in "I love you" if "I" loves all without knowing what it is to love any *one* "you". "Am I free? Or numb?"

There's wonder about the space between the breath and the walls and how it can be so when air so naturally wants to fill up a room. Suspended in a belief for love that is universal; a longing to understand love that is personal. This temple's not empty...there's air inside.

"Children are dying" as a virtue signal with no further critical analysis or call to action reads: "I feel bad but taking stances makes me uncomfortable bc I don't wanna offend anyone! Let's go with something so general that everyone can get on board!" All words, no change.

Yes, children dying is tragic. People dying is tragic. Peace is good. Evil is bad. The sky is blue. The world is round.

Mitigation: Challenge the neutrality and ineffectiveness of your or others' sympathies (can be gently). "Offending no one" means not changing the status quo.
If you even care at all, have a stance. And do something about it. Platitudes are just that. "Thoughts and prayers" don't do shit. What are you doing about it?

Things you can do Re: Israel-Palestine/Gaza/generally:
- Develop media literacy + educate yourself
- Take an actual stance
- Share your stance
- Find calls to action (activism groups are helpful for this info and there are many on social media) & actually act, and share the info

Imagine if politics was run by people who value peace, justice, and prosperity for all instead of corruption, greed, imperialism, and special interests. It's speaking in pure ideals but if all "peaceful" people check out (a privilege, tbh)...what does that leave for leadership?

The scope of a "peaceful" person:

"I'm a nice person, I have a good life, I'm at peace" can all be true. However, w/o thought or awareness for what's happening outside your life or to others, the only peace you care about is your own. Wrong? No. Incomprehensive? Likely.

The scope of a "comprehensively peaceful" person: 

"I'm at peace, and I want to share it with others. Others deserve peace too. My peace gives me strength. How can I, in this position of strength, grow the peace that we experience as a society?"

Crowd-sourcing wisdom:

How do you balance reigning in your own ego with "This is intolerable"?

Where does a generally calm person that lets many things go and keeps themselves in check draw the line? Where does their responsibility end? Where does the other person's start?

The simple answer is when boundaries are violated. Where and how do you draw these boundaries? What about for situations that are "less intense" (eg. not outright abuse but negligence, ignorance, inconsideration, or avoidance)?

What's the line between "I am unconditionally loving" vs. "This is BS. I deserve better"? "I deserve" can be a fxn of the ego. Pointing out "unfairness" and cutting people off can be a fxn of the ego. What is loving vs. not? What is being kind vs. not being taken advantage of?

"8 Rules of Love" by Jay Shetty "Love Again and Again" thesis:
If you spend your time focusing on why you're not getting love, you'll feel a shortage of it. If you just simply give it freely where you can, it finds its way reflected back to you. https://t.co/Nev49940Mh

The second part isn't meant to sound transactional either. A giving and loving mindset will have you experiencing more love and joy overall. A reflection within. And it might come back to you (statistically) but that's not the intention. It's not giving or loving with expectation
A fixation on receiving love has you looking at how you are or are not getting it instead of creating it yourself. It's far less powerful. It seeks to consume, take...not create, and give

I think about this article a lot. It details there are three categories of people: givers, takers, and matchers. Within the giver population, there are "smart givers" (distinct from matchers). This article posits that smart givers succeed the most

https://t.co/MuklgEEV5z
My addendum: If givers are on a spectrum, then on one end there's poor boundaries/high enablement of others and on the other end there's healthy boundaries/high agency.

The essence of purest giving that is successful for you and others (aka benefits all, and isn't self-destructive) is effortless compassion, but knowing where or when to stop: the "stop" criteria is if it enables or promotes a decrease in compassion within the other party.

Side note: I understand the irony of "succeed the most" as sounding like weird framing when talking about givers. Because these givers *aren't* focused on personal success as the goal when they give lol. The nature of being giving, at its best, is that it's simply not for you

Enablement is conditioning the recipient of your giving to have dependency when there's capability instead for empowerment. Decrease in compassion looks like conditioning the recipient to taking you or others for granted, or normalizing ingratitude and/or entitlement

Being value-based applies to finding peace in not being liked by everyone.

Eg. the condescending intellectual or the one going around saying "I don't like most people! Earn my love" don't think I'm cool? I value humbleness and inclusivity - does approval matter from *you*? Nah
When you know your values, you especially won't care for approval from people with values that diametrically oppose yours and it's freeing.

As you realize significant discrepancies in values, you'll naturally find your time and energy shifting away from those who are not for you, and you'll have more mental bandwidth for finding new connections that have you feeling seen for what do you value.
(And I'm saying all this, but ironically I do generally feel liked and respected by the people currently in my life. I guess I talk to younger me a lot haha)

*what you do value

Why IS it that Buddhism is way more popular than Taoism or Vedanta in western audiences?

My thoughts for at least being more popular than Vedanta is that it's compatible with western Christianity because there is no other God concept (Buddha is an enlightened human and teacher but not God) so it's palatably compatible
Vs. Vedanta is Hinduism which is generalized to mean Vaishnavism and therefore polytheism which is incompatible with western Christianity's one-God model

This isn't saying do everything sober. It's saying do this mental litmus test first. Do you really want to hang out with that group tonight? Do you really want to go to that concert/thing?

Enjoy things you already like first for a more fulfilled life

This is an attachment style model but I recall using it two years ago to filter through checking myself on how I viewed friendships. If I felt some kind of way I either considered reworking my self-perception or how I perceived that friend https://t.co/vOfutR9wST

Basically I went:
- Where am I on these coordinates?
- Do I like myself enough? That must exist first
- Do I like this friend enough? Do I have them on a pedestal? Or do I have an unfair distaste?

If stuck in the Dismissing quadrant with someone, I'd "dismiss them" aka deprioritize/let them go

If I moved to the Secure quadrant with those questions and examination, then that's a good thing

Anything else: The mission is to develop more self-love before acting

Overexposure will make you take something magical for granted. My friends who work for Disney get themselves and friends into Disneyland for free and seem sick of that place because they're always taking people. The people they take, on the other hand, are in wonder

Desensitization is mitigated through intentionally tracking how you're spending your time so there's not "too much" of any one thing. If something you once loved is starting to lose its magic you can cut back and you either realize you don't miss it, or you appreciate it again
Life is lived better when the experiences and moments you fill it with are not ones that you take for granted

Forget rating people out of 10. Rate platonic relationships out of 20 and romantic interests out of 20 + 10 = 30:
• Personality/10 (platonic, dating)
• Shared values/10. +1 for each major shared value, -1 for each (platonic, dating)
• Physical attractiveness/10 (dating only)

Do a weighted average and watch physical 9s become weighted 4s. Understand why you find your "nice" friend unfulfilling to hang out with--maybe they're a nice person but they don't add up in shared values. And watch those scoring high in personalities + values  become beautiful

You hear personality matters all the time - it does. I used to put weight into personality AND physical attractiveness both on the dating front, especially, until I realized values drive a critical analysis of personality facets as well, and serve as a counterweight/fit check

It's silly to deny that physical attraction matters on the dating front. But theoretically a 20/30 in personality (damn near impossible) and values destroys a 10/30 (damn near impossible too) in looks. The more you admire someone for personality + values, the less looks matter

*-1 for each diametrically opposed value !!!!!!!

You're only as forgiving of others as you are of yourself. If you're especially unforgiving of someone, it's because you're angry at some part of yourself for that experience too and you're unforgiving of your end of it.

Comparisons I'm unlearning as I confront my ego around anger:
- "I never get angry" is not better than recognizing and feeling anger, and is probably false/a sign you repress anger
- Passive aggression is not any better than aggression
- "I'm sad" is not better than "I'm angry"

- Someone who "never gets angry" isn't better than someone who does (again, it's probably repression in the former)
- Silent out-letting of anger (eg. Journaling) isn't actually a physical vent and is not better than physically venting (eg. Shouting it out when no one is around)

Relational things I'm learning:
- An ego insisting "I don't get angry" had me mistakenly believing (subconsciously) that I was better than others who don't

- When someone finally saw my outward anger they quickly forgave me for it and I was surprised. Because of this, I forgave myself quicker and I forgave all their anger too. I realized the resentment and judgement for myself and another that had been built up, blocking forgivenes

- The judging part of me that judged my own anger wanted to destroy me. So when I saw anger in others, this part wanted them destroyed too. Without judgment, I can accept others and others more unconditionally. I can't love when inhibited by fear. Without the fear, I can love.

@leverage_____ It is. I think it works the other way around too. When someone forgives you for something that you feel like you can't forgive yourself for, you realize that kind of forgiveness is possible and you can forgive yourself and others for those things
@CharleyTodd Aww thanks!

- As I unlock memories, repression of anger probably started with early childhood punishment by a parent if I got angry. It's healing to see that anger now and to tell myself it's okay to exist, to let it be, and vent it, and to hear another tell me it's okay too.

- It's hurtful and not okay to take anger out on someone (passive aggression or pure aggression, doesn't matter) but in the (self and other) forgiveness process I am no longer denouncing or shaming anyone (myself or another) if it happens in the process. We're human

- If you're angry as a result of an interaction with someone, genuinely and fully venting it away from them is an act of love. You can return to them and talk through the hurt after blowing steam. But that initial solo venting can be the healthy thing. The process can all be love

- Even passive aggression towards someone means that there's anger in you that needs to be seen, processed, and released - it can also benefit from you solo venting. It's still anger. Just because it's "softer" than direct aggression doesn't mean that it's not *just* as harmful
@cordy_0 Power to you ☺️ Doing the work can be challenging but so worth it

No Bad Parts excerpt (p. 139) addressing a pitfall of spiritual bypassing in meditative practice and spiritual tradition https://t.co/uAGtSuougU
I've observed a lot of "ego bashing" in communities studying spirituality. From an IFS perspective, the ego arises from parts that are simply trying to help you and mean well

Reflections as someone believing in the non-dual: 

Bypass: "That's not me, I'm Spirit/bliss" -> indirectly pushes parts/emotions away to a corner -> repression

Vs.

Integration: "I see your pain, and honor it. Ty" -> pain feels seen by the Spirit in you -> acceptance -> healing

In other words, the ego isn't your enemy. It's a friend (or a group of parts - friends). Pause and talk to it. It's trying to tell you something bc it is still you and it's looking out for you, and needs a true friend to talk to and hear it. That true friend is in your Spirit 💕

I'm curious how much we operate in etymological bias, celebrating the light over dark when the dark is also equally beautiful.

Eg.
White witch
Light worker 
Elves with fair hair 
Seeing the light

We don't celebrate dark nearly as much.
The "shadow" is the one that hurts.

Dark can be beautiful like the void in Buddhism, emptiness, nothingness.
The serenity of the night sky.
The shade is cool and relief from the heat of the sun. The shadow is a friend.

What if darkness didn't imply inherent negativity any more or less than light?

If you handled darkness with equal rhetoric, suddenly nothing is inherently bad or evil, it just is. You release self-judgement. Your own light and dark are both equally beautiful. And then it begs the question, which is which?

*also insert racial implications of how fairies, elf queens, ethereal healers, angels, and any kind of "sweet loving good" will have many automatically picturing a Caucasian woman bc we think "light"*

Learning/practicing from Aletheia unfolding: At one's core is peace and presence. It's the rejection of anything (a thought or feeling that enters) that takes one out of this. With awareness, acceptance, and gratitude ("Welcome, not trying to change you, & thank you") they soften

Applying how the same can be applied relationally for harmony between you and others: Genuinely welcoming them, letting them be in whatever state they are w/o trying to change them, gratitude towards them for being as they are. Often preserves your peaceful state & softens theirs

The caveat here is that you must first be in a grounded state for this. In theory and practice, as you practice this acceptance/gratitude with your inner world, it gets easier to get and stay grounded, and (after offering it to yourself) easier to offer this energy to others too

This process is distinct from spiritual bypass because you are actually seeing, listening to, and acknowledging the emotions and thoughts and thanking them for letting you know what they want you to (acceptance) vs. pushing them away by going "That's not my nature" (rejection)
In short, compassion for another + yourself at the same time in escalating conflict is kindly communicating your boundaries and sticking by them

I continue to marvel at how healthily naming and processing anger as an emotion is groundbreaking for moving through pain, especially if you grew up as a "nice girl" & anger was "forbidden". Excerpts from an @artofaccomp podcast below illustrating this concept are threaded here

From https://t.co/gSPdUu9Bzx

"Client: I thought there was a way around it. I thought I could find the root, the fear behind the anger and process it as sadness because sadness is safe for women in society. I’ve been like fuck yeah, I am going to cry all the fucking time."

"Joe: If you are crying all the time, and it is not resolving, it is because there is another emotion there you aren’t feeling. There is this theory that’s like all of your anger is sadness or hurt or something like that. Bullshit. It’s true and it’s not true."
"Joe: It’s true in the fact that yes, underneath all of the anger is hurt, but it is also true that underneath all of the hurt is anger."

"Client: I was so hell bent on proving to my coach I didn’t have anger that I found a neuroscience article about this, about how sadness counteracts anger and maybe it can be felt that way. She responded with what part of yourself needs to prove right now that you are not angry."

"Joe: When you suppress your anger, you lose your fire. You lose your determination. You lose your clarity. That’s how it works, but you don’t have to get angry at people. You don’t have to start yelling at people."

(alluding to moving anger - venting but not directing at ppl)

The irony of loneliness is how universal it is. The more people I get to know better, the more I realize everyone has pain and to be alone with it all the time is a lonely experience. People don't want to be alone with their pain all the time; this is universal and understated

We carry pain and no one else can carry it for us or make it go away, although others might be around to support us. At the end of the day, though, it's you going to bed in the quiet moments of the night as the sole being that can carry that pain, and those moments are lonely.

When you see and accept your pain without trying to change it, it understands that it has you to hold it. When it knows it has you, it can soften. And when you extend this same acceptance to others and their pain, you create bridges between your lonely hearts.

"It's okay to be sad."
"You can cry. It's okay to cry."
"You don't have to hurt alone."
"I don't need you to be any other way right now."

When you speak to your pain like this, your pain is less alone.
When you speak to others in pain like this, they are less alone with pain.

Tl;dr you don't have to solve or change your pain or other peoples' pain. You can just be with each, and with compassion.

We already are alone with pain more often than not; to extend company and compassion therefore makes this universal loneliness a little less lonely.

Does anyone have oddly poignant memories from childhood media that ring in your head as an adult and then  you transpose their wisdom? Example:

"When you need me but do not want me, then I must stay. When you want me but no longer need me, then I have to go." - Nanny McPhee

This is interesting to turn over in my head. It makes sense as a children's movie quote on parent-child relationships bc it's an adult being a parental figure & ultimately guiding children to develop agency. The kids go from bratty and ungrateful to empowered and grateful.

I wonder what the adult-to-adult relationship version would be (friendships or romantic) bc there's a difference between caring and caretaking between two adults.

"When you need me but do not want me, then I must go. When you want me but no longer need me, then ____."

Thoughts?

I think about this like leaving another adult to do their own work. As much as you care about another adult, you can't  do the work for them. The distinction is that they're not children / you're not their parent. They have to find their love and gratitude in their own way

I would still say the OG quote applies in situations of selfless service and empowerment ("Teaching a man to fish instead of catching his fish"), but not to romantic relationships or friendships where you're sacrificing yourself and in a caretaker mode instead of caring mode

Related: Art of Accomplishment did a great podcast episode on this called "Care over Caretaking - Loving without Losing Yourself"

https://t.co/b3mgO9bVc7

For those that stayed when adults (not children!!) don't want you but you feel like they "need you" and are unlearning that

Threading helpful notes from a Tantrik Yoga kula/study group I recently joined!

(Note: Tantrik Yoga is NOT tantric sex lol. It's completely separate. It's spiritual practice aka yoga for the non-renunciates i.e. those who are not monks, nuns, etc)

[Pure motive] Notes on 3 common, misaligned spiritual motives & their antidotes:

1) Practice not "to fix something wrong with yourself" -> Practice out of love for yourself 

2) Pactice not to experience "spiritual bliss states" -> Practice to uncover the Truth of what we are

3) Practice not to increase power or control/for the spiritual ego -> Practice for the benefit of all beings

Putting this all together, here's an intention-setting prayer:

"May I practice out of love for myself with a desire to know the Truth, for the benefit of all beings."

I've lived cerebrally for a while. In my loneliest teenage years I'd contextualize life & death like that of the stars and supernovas, as a fractal of one grand existence, and say "I am here to write a beautiful story in the story of all in a universe of creation and destruction"

This was after understanding the concept of panentheism and long before hearing about nondualism and imagination served me well for a long time. I'd lie in bed and pretend I was made of stars. The sun was my friend. So were flowers, and silly little character drawings I made.

A few weeks ago, I caught myself writing mental poetry, yet again about the spider in my car being a friend - and I went "Wait, Nadia, you have friends. You don't *have* to do that anymore."

But still, sometimes I do. And the mind feels like a beautiful little palace BUT

More recently I'm realizing that there's a balance between imagination and escapism. It's called "grounding" because you have a cord to the earth too. It's not just the mind that's consciousness communing with the cosmos at the crown, but the body also communing with the earth.

A music psychotherapist led a beautiful guitar/grounding meditation this weekend where she had us visualize roots growing from where our feet were planted and I realized it's too often I identify with the stars and not enough with the earth. I got the urge to go lay in grass

And realized that it's never been about just the mind, or just the body, but the body-mind this whole time. That the body-mind in the here and now is a column connecting earth to cosmos and truth to consciousness and beyond & I'll float aimlessly without roots to ground it down

I've felt emotions *heavily* in my body my whole life so it's thrilling and a little scary to have these realizations to really work on grounding and see them instead of poetically write them off as a bypass.

Now where in LA can I go lie on grass and breathe with the earth? 💚

Tl;dr living cerebrally and imagination is good but not at the expense of living in your body (as a form of bypass). The mind connects to the cosmos and the body connects to earth. I want to feel connected to the stars and the earth at the same time. Working on the latter!

It's wild how two people can go from lovers to complete strangers. The experience of seeing a former photo and feeling like you just imagined it all is so disorienting. There's this recognition confusion. "Who was that? What even was that? What happened? Did it happen at all?"

The absence of someone means a present version of them is imperceptible. You recall a version of them that's either a past version or the fantasy version of them built on your hopes for the future (at one point). You might realize any concept of them you can recall isn't reality

And it's here when I go "Did I imagine it all?" I'm all for feeling all & I try to not repress, yet this feeling is perplexing. Here once, gone now, yet still somewhere. Frames of consciousness in endless flow through space-time. Schrödinger's ghosts; are we each gone or alive?

The Cosmic Cradle: How to feel love when your heart hurts

I get why they say to go touch grass. I slow down to remember that the earth is beneath me. I'm used to living in my head, so I recruit imagination to grow roots from where my body contacts the ground down into the earth.

In this union, the body becomes real, inescapable. Emotions become tangible. I breathe with the earth.

I wonder if you think of me when the light of the sun hits the leaves in the trees - those still, golden moments.

I let my heart be sad over not being able to share this light. I claim my sad: "This is my sad. It's all mine (hell yeah). I get to hold it, and the earth holds me"

And when I understand this, I am reclaimed because I understand I do not belong to you.

The heart is a cosmic cradle for the emotions, as the body is for the heart, and as the earth is for the body. No, I do not belong to you - I belong to this earth, and to this life. Here I am real, held, and loved infinitely.

Does anyone have good resources to work through the painful toxicity of experiencing one-sided jealousy/one-sided competition in former friendships or relationships? I try to practice mindfulness and holistic healing, but this has been in my system hurting for a hot minute

Elaboration on the one-sided scenario:

Them: Envied what you have, put you down in many subtle ways while imitating you, one-sidedly competed, shut you down

You: All you wanted was for them to thrive too and you were on their team the whole time, and it was never a competition

Maybe preaching to the choir here, but rarely is it the complete picture to believe in binaries, opposites, or antonyms. Multiple experiences in the entire range of expansion (+) and contraction (-) can exist in a given moment. Happiness and sadness can exist simultaneously

Eg. being relieved that you and someone were finally honest with each other (+) but sad that it temp(?) changed your dynamic (-), and grateful for the challenge and reflection arising from bluntness (+), while being disappointed by the bluntness (-) and it's all somehow ok & good

Eg. Watching a sunset and noticing the sadness you hold in your heart about the time that you did this with someone by your side (-) but also entirely grateful for experiencing these moments of solitude and no one is here *including them* (+) and also "damn this is gorgeous" (+)

List goes on but "I'm not sad, I'm happy" & "I'm not happy, I'm sad" don't make sense to me anymore. It's "Happiness is here. Sadness is here too" as an example of one "binary". Insert anger, love, fear, anxiety, & countless other emotions, and a whole set of permutations follow

Hareesh on the View of Tantrik yoga (Nondual Shaiva Tantra) which affirms that the essence-nature of everything is Divine (Advaitans, see: Brahman) and everything manifests from a field of infinite potential and is reabsorbed back into it (this plays well w/ the concept of Maya) https://t.co/F8oBht6XjF

The Central Channel (sushumnā nāḍī) is the physio-energetic axis of experience aka the core of the "subtle body"/"energy body" and includes the seven major chakras that everyone's heard about (too long to cover in a post but this information is available ubiquitously)

The center point for consciousness is in the head for most. One can repose the center to a still point at the base of the heart (search "heart center" or "nila bindu" meditations) - the energetic center.  From here it's easier to observe thoughts without accepting or denying them

Bindu refers to essence-points in the body. There are two more bindus. The Red Bindu is the lower-body bindu and is half-way between the 2nd and 3rd chakras. It's the center point of embodiment for the human experience and for now I've learned grounding with it (next post)

Grounding via Red Bindu: While seated, sense the Red Bindu (low-belly). Focus attention deep into the earth. Inhale earth energy up & into your body, offer it into the Red Bindu. At the end of the inhale, tone the pelvic floor. Exhale but retain tone. Then release tone. Repeat

I'm learning for exercises like the above, you have to decide the time amount that's right for you LOL. Instruction says do it for 3-5 min for less activation, 10-15 for more but in the beginning I could only do it for a couple min before going to normal breathing/no toning

And this grounding practice can feel sexual (as this bindu is the center for embodiment & creative and sexual energy) so you have to adjust based on how much grounded or sexual energy you already have as a baseline and that's different for everyone. General goal: to feel grounded

Learned about the side channels (running from 3rd eye to pelvic floor) of the body in Side Channel theory

Left channel (ida nadi):  Associated with inhale, moon, feminine, inner knowing & reflecting

Right channel (pingala nadi): Associated with exhale, sun, masculine, action

The left channel is visualized as white and the right is visualized as red. To feel side channels, can do a Hamsa meditation (searchable)

Personal anecdote: I struggled with the left/right/inhale/exhale so I grasped lil totems as mnemonics in each hand to help. Can share more!

Lately I've been enjoying leaning into the "magical" and intuitive side of life more. A healing arts-music festival I was supposed to attend got cancelled bc of a fire last Sat. So instead, my friend found a soundbath that led us to a nat'l yoga day celebration in a park (cont)

where we meet this Ayurveda practitioner and his school's community. I'm asking him all these questions. Without even missing a beat he goes "you're an engineer so..." (I didn't even tell him this?!). My friend and I heard a lot of perspective we felt we needed to hear that day

& I learn this practitioner's clinic is literally in my neighborhood with the exact community I've been looking for. Also, as I observe people like this read me, I internalize that intuition is and always has been here. I'm starting to truly listen to and sharpen mine as well

Also, this friend and I didn't really know each other well before that day but we learned about overlaps in our existential views & rly connected. She felt the fire was fated. & we both got approached by squirrels & a butterfly on a wholesome earth-&-spirit woo wavelength LOL

And afterwards we go to one of those metaphysical supply stores bc she's into tarot. I was a former skeptic but I realize a lot of these things are *tools* for you and your imagination to make a narrative based on your intuition & drive the bodymind to decide its path in healing

Eg. I'm just there in the store touching crystals for fun and there's a small black heart shaped one that I decide feels like it'll pull out my anger and resentment and into itself when I hold it to meditate & a larger white heart one that I decide will remind me to output love

Is crystal magic real? Idk, can't tell you. That's your skepticism or joy to lean into. Are the imaginative and associative mnemonics my brain makes as it holds a form and constructs symbolism real? They drive my mind and body to meditative visualization, so works for me!

I used the crystals to help me with a Tantrik meditation where I struggled to remember left/right with in-down/out-up (had it backwards until I gripped them & could feel the size differentials and then recall color) - dark for absorb + purify negative, light for breath out light

tl;dr is that I had a magical lil weekend where I'm fascinated by intuition. I'm enjoying the magic of belief and imagination. I'm even finding my imagination is helping me connect to my body more (something I've struggled with before) & excited to explore it :D

As someone who often leans imagination-heavy, I found that having actual physical objects to hold with contrast and symbolic associations was a good physical grounder/reminder to stay focused on and in the body

You can catch yourself when you move through life feeling like you have to prove that what you create or do is "enough" by noticing patterns of over-explaining to yourself and to others - this arises from that internal monologue & the little voice going "This counts, right?"

You don't have to prove anything to me, him, her, or anyone else. It's enough, it counts ❤️ But don't take it from me or them. It's never really about proving anything to anyone; it's about believing it yourself first. And then, if others don't value it, at least you know you do

The above entails this chronology:
1) creating or doing in a way that you do genuinely value and then 
2) believing in that inherent value regardless of others

If you aren't creating or doing in a personally meaningful way, it's going to be hard to believe it yourself first
@christineist LOL i like this but maybe without the "have to" and instead "an inspired, potential choice"

You can put fulfilment into context:
- Imitation (eg. copying a painting to learn) may be initially fulfilling in the technical/mind-sense
- Originality may be more fulfilling long-term in the emotional/heart-sense
Both have places in the sharing process!

What follows this is naturally a balance of fulfilment/validation. The more heart-sense fulfilment & self-validation you have, the less technical "perfect" or "good enough" matters because it means something to *you*. No baseline for comparison; YOU created & set the standard :)

Example: I pulled up a floral painting to copy and learn & "good enough"/"right" become more of a thing w/ a standard - not as emotionally satisfying, but good for learning. But when I paint my feelings straight from imagination I feel free and happy & idc if ppl think it's good

The point of this thread btw isn't to obsess over logic so much as illustrate how traditions all kinda pointing to the same thing 

Could add Theravada, Mahayana, Tibetian Buddhism, Sufism, & even Abrahamic religion (but those equations would be more complex)

Bc when I hear that Tantrikas and Advaitans have historical disagreements I'm like ...how? Why? It's gotta be a gatekeeping/pride/personal ritual thing over an existential view thing. I love studying both

Anything I call magic is the mnemonic effect of real objects bringing concepts to the forefront of thought & intuition to create somatic effects/focus in the bodymind. It's still "magic" to me 

I don't rly subscribe to jyotish or astrology so pls don't put me in that bucket :')

Or like tarot, crystals, etc. But ok what does woo do?

Suppose a friend does a tarot reading & what she mentions abt career resonates in your head & stays w you

Your body & mind latch onto where your intuition wants to focus next & makes meaning, & naturally discards the rest

Plus my friends who rly like the woo are so cute & energetic and i mirror their energy

& I'd do a ouija board bc I like using my imagination to give myself chills but no I don't believe evil spirits are actually there tho I can vibe like it (but no one agrees to do one w me 🥲)

But NO not every girl in LA believes in astrology like that ok
(And it's okay if they do!)
Also nondualistic spirituality for me comes from logic and experiential moments through contemplation, meditation, and psychedelic-assisted ego death/dissolution

I know I share a lot of woo, magical, and romantic stuff so my purpose to elaborate on this is to shed light on the epistemic basis and critical thinking involved behind the adoption of these beliefs 💖

I see a lot of self-improvement spirals on here. A lot of unintentional and well-intended addiction to continuous improvement. You're exactly where you need to be. You have time. Understand what you value but let go of the narratives and the need for control & the need to *know*

Live your life by your own timeline and You have everything you need in the now, and as you move through life in each moment, trust that you'll know how to keep moving through it. Maybe preaching to the choir, but continuous wanting will have you rejecting what you do have now

Decouple from the narratives of what happiness looks like to you in the distant future. You don't have to have all the answers. You can know what happiness *in this moment* looks like if you genuinely know your values and move intentionally. Sometimes that's all you need

Seriously, the neuroticism that comes with excessive self-improvement focus and comparison and competition and spiritual materialism (again, all well-intended!) isn't worth it if you're not enjoying life and if you're unable to have gratitude for what's right in front of you

You don't always have to say smart things or build brilliant things. No one's saying don't be driven to create and by all means pour passion into areas where it thrives, but not at the expense of gratitude and peace
A lot of peace is attained simply through relinquishing control and trusting yourself to make a value-aligned move at any given moment

@nomanautomata @christineist And also if you never take the time to become relatively emotionally available! Then it won't matter *who* it is (speaking from personal experience on both sides)

You're not entitled to someone's vulnerability. Vulnerability is reserved for environments with trust that the other person will respond supportively. If you get the small talk™ version of someone it can very well be bc you haven't established trust - not bc they lack depth

People can completely stop opening up when the response they're met with is minimizing or unsolicited advice-giving for even a small thing, and as a result they definitely won't trust you next time with the big things. This can be rectified by developing genuine empathy

You're a safe space only when your ability to empathize & hold space for others expressing even small things is genuine. I've seen people try to encourage vulnerability by sharing first themselves but it's all about how you respond when they share, not "I share, why can't you"

You can and will say ignorant things in your lifetime. We're imperfect, human, & can't know everything. Saying ignorant things isn't the problem. Not being able or willing to learn in the moment might be, though. Can't go wrong if you're operating from curiosity & kindness

It's all in the responses, and on both ends. 

The ones who elucidate ignorance: Do you do it gently, assuming good intentions, and with the purpose of awareness and teaching? Gentleness, humanization, and a demeanor of raising awareness is effective. Shaming someone is not

The one who said something ignorant: Do you get defensive and ashamed, or do you stay open-minded and take the opportunity to learn? "Oh dang, thanks, actually I didn't consider that" has this lovely  approachability and it's far more eloquent than digging yourself in deeper
Ignorance isn't inherently evil, it's "I didn't know" - the antidote is awareness. The response of both parties (the one expressing, the one pointing out ignorance) is character-defining

I think it's natural & well-intended for those discovering purpose & self-love to want to help people as a means of finding purpose. It's beautiful, even. At some point you'll benefit from doing this reflection of whether it's sometimes a projection of you trying to save yourself

Consider asking yourself:
1) Do I truly, truly listen to others with equal respect, reverence, and empathy? Is it a collaborative approach?
2) Does doing this make me feel like I'm low-key "better" (wiser, kinder, etc) or "more important" than other people?

I'll leave it there

This isn't saying stop doing good. Please (continue to) do your good! Just don't forget to pause and reflect sometimes and especially if you want to increase the purity of your service - the best help comes when it's truly focused on the other, and not low-key on you

Sooo I realize I can be overeager and impatient sometimes to grow faster than a process, and a little arrogantly insistent on my own process over conventional wisdom until i am checked & check myself. Had a mini humbling moment yesterday (story time!)

I attended a lecture on the Vedanta guru/ishta/mantra process a couple of weeks ago and spoke to the lead swami about guidance. There's a prep stage first. He asked if I read and I say "Yes!!" & he's like "Long books? 1000 pages?" & I go "...maybe medium" so i got a "medium" rec

So I grab the books, and the medium book turns out to be decently long still. And then I learn my temple friend had gotten a shorter rec for that step bc she wanted to go through the process faster. Later, I emailed the swami asking for a shorter reading rec option in preparation

And he replied kindly and gave me the shorter rec too but doubled down on the initial longer rec. And at first my initial thought was like "Whyyy the other person got the shorter reading" and my knee-jerk reaction was "Maybe this is old-fashioned; maybe he's not my teacher"

But then I paused. Something told me to trust the intuition of a teacher. I realized "Here, you're coming to someone who devoted his life & for guidance but now you're resisting it. It's not that much more labor, it just takes a little longer - if you rly care, why rush?"

It was easy watching Avatar as a kid going "Aang wtf are you doing dummy" as he's too excited to firebend and doesn't wanna listen but i realize... oh shoot. Sometimes, that's me. & I didn't get a shorter assignment. I got short option + the OG assignment *both* in his reply LOL

And it reflected the shortcuts i've tried to take my whole life - this tendency for me to go "i get it, i understand now!" before i slow down and listen and try to really understand. These were calm realizations as I internalized that a personal trial of mine is this discipline

I did NOT like to study or sit down with a textbook or homework in school or in college. I'd mostly cram and just show up to tests

Yes, I like to read and be curious but only on *my* whim. So in a weird, magical way this feels like a necessary first step?

Like idk even now it feels so unfamiliar to me but maybe that's the point. Reverence for wisdom and trust in the process. yeah you can be free-spirited and energetic but if you're coming to learn from someone i realize you can't over-zealously be like "I know better"

As a kid I'd get annoyed at my mom for "Bengali-fying" recipes. "Mom this was bread rolled with Italian sausage, why'd you make it with jeera & green chili??" She'd say she made it better; I'd roll my eyes. Now I live across the country seeking out fusion food & feeling nostalgic

I've shed the third-culture kid shame around "brownness" & grown to embrace culture since. I'm lucky that LA is a melting pot with a diverse & innovative food scene & I find fusion food especially fun to explore. Fusion is a testament to the ABCD experience & reminiscent of home

Authentic Bengali food feels like a hug from home & my mom's food feels like she's here with me. I flew back to LA a couple of days ago and she insisted I take keema chili bread (always delicious, btw). It's her love language and it has her crafty, intuitive, one-of-one signature

These days many hype places *are* fusiony spots in LA, offering fresh takes on classics, novelty, and vibrant flavor. In all this I embrace my mom and her natural creativity in the kitchen. I'm inspired and thrilled to experiment in the kitchen and learn from the best ❤️

20s are almost over & I'm in the process of actively letting go of this dream I've had of settling down with someone and having kids. I cannot get attached to this life path like it's *the one* that'll bring me happiness. Would I like it? Yes. Is it guaranteed? Technically, no

Letting go doesn't mean giving up on dreams. I'll still do my best to plan for the option to have a family. It's still a dear thought

It's an exercise to develop a deep okayness w/ life playing out completely different than everything I had dreamed of & be at peace with it all

Awk, I just started crying after posting this haha

This isn't a pity post, it's just a reflection on really having to change my thinking 

No matter what, I want to be okay with having me and only me & trust everything else meant for me will come later (whatever it looks like)

The exercise I've been doing:
"If you never fall in love again, will you be okay? If you're single for the rest of your life, will you be okay? If you never have kids, will you be okay?"

& if the answer to any of the above is no, figure out what I need for "yes"
@brimmingvessel Thank you for these words Matilda! 🤍 Practicing the detachment in the hypothetical scenarios definitely is emotional but what I feel is healthy in the long-run

And then, ofc, because I believe in full agency and creating life, this is happening in parallel: "What needs to happen for me to be in healthy relationships? What can I do right now?"

& do it without outcome attachment or single-point fixation, and trust in my own timing :)

Caring about politics and activism isn't mutually exclusive with a spiritual path or attaining and maintaining inner peace - beyond individual liberation it even supports the systemic scalability of attitudes, action, and public policy for collective liberation https://t.co/KcJLxQ27D9

Food for thought: If you're jarred by news so hard that you totally check out, refuse to have a stance, or dismiss participation, it's an energetic boundary/skill issue

(No one's saying consume graphic media though, that can be a lot)

I can understand when people are turned off by vitriol & condemnation - regardless of how checked in or out of politics you are, the rules for productive and inviting discourse are applicable. But painting activists with a broad brush of "angry & noisy" is a lack of understanding

Photo context (Nov 2022): "Pro-democracy protesters scuffled with riot police in Bangkok a few hundred meters from the venue for the Asia Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC) summit, where leaders from the U.S., China and 19 other Pacific Rim nations are meeting." (cont)

"Protesters spray painted riot police shields and held up placards calling for Prayuth to resign. They also demanded the release of political prisoners and reform of Thailand’s lese majeste law, which carries a sentence of up to 15 years in prison for insulting or defaming (cont)

the royal family."

If you have an aversion to being checked into politics or protests and you consider yourself mindfully aware, I challenge you to understand what monks actually did.

Thich Nhat Hanh was extremely vocal about the Vietnam War, buddies with MLK, and unpopular with the US military. He coined Engaged Buddhism. “When bombs begin to fall on people, you cannot stay in the meditation hall all of the time.”

This isn't a thread telling anyone what to do or care about, or shaming anyone for whatever level of participation, but it is posing the challenge of how you apply these aspirations of awareness and peace - is it for *just you* despite the rest of the world, or for everyone?
@justavagrant_ awwwww!!!!!

Footnote: I acknowledge shaming by some "activists" is a turnoff. Shame isn't helpful for motivating ppl to care. You'll find just as many people living by "it's about calling people in, not calling them out." Just like sports fans, there's good & bad vibe activists. C'est la vie

I know this is a temporary state. It comes in waves and it's passing. But it's a painful process to get someone else's voice out of my head and having to do so solo. This is someone who doesn't really specifically & sincerely apologize for the things they've said and done

And at this point I don't need or want him to. But then it becomes on me to move myself through this eg. tell myself "Nadia, remember when you were ignored for two decades but ppl still liked your thoughts? You are more than your appearance. Don't listen to what he said"

Literally so many things got reduced or attributed to my appearance by someone but thank gosh I grew up "not-cute but interesting" or w/e so I KNOW I have substance (& what the fuck is wrong with having style now too?? Nothing) but I constantly have to explain this to myself now

There's still so much rage and hurt. So much. I never thought like "people just want to be my friend or engage with me on twitter bc pretty" until you came along. Never. GTFO of my head. Imposter syndrome shoved down my throat. I am trying so hard to detox from it now

This shit made me stop tweeting for a while, change my pfp to a doodle before changing it back, made me go "maybe i shouldn't wear makeup around Twitter friends" before pausing and having to correct myself going "wait WHY, if I do that how about we all just wear potato sacks"

And this isn't even getting into other things with this person (those are far more interpersonal and relational, it gets dark) but I notice this whole imposter syndrome one pop up the most frequently and actively as I tweet and I feel that anger

Even now I'm like "should I change my pfp to a picture of a cartoon daisy with a smiley face on it" and there's this "you deserve nothing" voice looping in my head BUT FUCKING WHY. It wasn't there before you. it wasn't. Ik it's not true too

Thanks for letting me vent, y'all

And I'm not some victim or whatever, I have agency, I work on myself, but I have so much anger about having to work through imposter syndrome that feels like it was injected into my veins by someone only in the last year or so

This is someone who ended things and then stalked my Twitter from afar after, came back out of admiration, and seemed kinder at first but kept subtly constantly tearing me down over time. If I was wiser & stronger back then I wouldn't have ever entertained it again but...learned

People vastly underestimate the power of "placebo" and the term itself is often used to dismiss the power of the mind as it relates to the body. The mind and body are connected; your mind is part of your body and stress is the root cause for so many compounded physical ailments

Simplified example: If I give a first grader with testing anxiety a "magic rock" & tell her it helps with a test, & observe that her hands stop shaking during game time, & her body tenses less, & she does perform better, that IS real

I was this kid and I made my own "magic"

You can literally feel and do better for yourself by reducing stress

The chakra system provides a conceptual framework to establish practice and awareness such that body and mind can integrate better, incorporating the power of physical & visual mnemonics in understanding energy

You can literally imagine yourself into relaxed states. You can meditate into calm and literally reduce tension in your body, and if you have a visual imagination you can start to see symbols that represent what's happening in your body and in your life

And because of how it's used in studies to equate to "ineffectual" or something with an "inactive" compound, "placebo" isn't even the word I ever want to use for things like this

It's the magic of the imagining, thinking, and conceptualizing mind as it relates to the bodymind

I want to do a long-form post (or maybe video) of the framework of magical reality that I do believe in. 

There's the fundamental Truth of existence, but beyond that, our mind largely creates and informs our realities. If that's the case, then why not ✨play✨

A movie that only you can watch, but it's just a compilation of every moment in time where you have said "I love you" to someone or someone has said "I love you" to you in your lifetime
You realize how many different human characters there actually are in it, and you smile just as much as you cry

You realize in how many moments these words are said in playfulness, how many on the brink of loss as you realize you took them for granted, how, how many were said that weren't even registered or seen, how many were deeply, deeply felt, the first & last times a voice will say it
Humanity's story of births and deaths and rebirths as a compilation of billions of these films
Your one film, you realize, is hours and hours long. Humanity's compilation spans an eternity

It's everything from a parent holding you when you're minutes old, to you and your drunk friends in Vegas that you may not even be friends with anymore, to all the love stories, to apologies, to that friend who teared up when it was exactly what they needed to hear in that moment

To the times when you said it because you felt like you had to, but you didn't mean it, and you were just being polite, to the times you couldn't mean it any more than you really really did in that exact moment
And to the times that you said it freely and happily without much thought around the room because you're in a state of sharing pure joy

I love how much art & poetry there is in Hinduism. The deities are metaphors for different universal concepts (but it's one Ultimate Reality aka God, wearing diff costumes) & play well with minds that appreciate anthropomorphizing the cosmos. It rly is "choose your own adventure"

Eg. The goddess Kali is terrifying as fuq (to me) in depictions but also described as motherly so there's this reverence for destruction, darkness, and mortality while also knowing you're deeply loved in & by the same world that destroys you. She's rly popular at my temple

That being said, i literally find Kali's art too scary, but instantly gravitated to the art of Nataraja (Shiva as the cosmic dancer) bc of his playful posture. He's a symbol for time and the cycles of creation and destruction in the universe and literally dancing through it
It's a reminder for me that everything in this world is a scintillating and fleeting manifestation of a larger, divine play and it's a reminder to me to dance and play

Different ppl feel called to different depictions. You can literally be like "my deity is SpongeBob" & have it be valid if somehow contemplating the symbolism of SpongeBob has you somehow experiencing and understanding you and everything is a manifestation of an Ultimate Reality

And that's still considered Hinduism if it leads you to this realization of "oneness" and divinity in you, others, and everything. Which is why I appreciate the fullness of it so much! At the core and in its purest forms, Hinduism is so unifying and accepting of all paths

@vibeshepherd Yesss pure Advaita is like this and one can definitely practice this way! I would add that most people with worldly desires aren't ready to practice just pure Advaita quite yet (they could be later in life, though!) and that's why I also appreciate incorporating tantra

If you're deep into mindfulness, meditation, and spirituality but you don't know your attachment style, I encourage checking yourself. In my experience, a spiritual practice can further reinforce an avoidant attachment style's tendency to spiritually bypass relational healing

Sure, life is easy and zen when you're meditating on your own and flowing through life. When you have a partner? You're going to realize different facets of you come out. You can't always exist in your own bubble when another's energy is in the equation

It blows my mind when people think "becoming spiritual" is the end-all-be-all and Eastern spirituality > Western psychology. "if I become realized it will solve my attachment issues in relationships"...no. no, it won't. It might help in various degrees, but it won't solve it all

Attachment theory and attachment work isn't BS or "unnecessary" just because you meditate, understand nondualism, have had mystical experiences, and are emotionally regulated *when you're on your own*

This is an endorsement for understanding and working on your attachment style in addition to your spiritual practice. It comes into play not just in romantic relationships but also in friendships and how you treat people in general

@innerpathing And be friends and pick the brains of your securely attached friends in healthy relationships. Understand what their dynamic looks like and how they each got there. See them as attachment/communication/standards role models :)

Do you know how incredible it feels to fully move on from a past relationship *without* rebounding??? I can do it??! Holy shit

Haven't been single for most of my 20s so this is something I needed to experience. I always tried to justify "this isn't a rebound" but they were (😬)

I stayed in things, fearing the pain of them ending, but to now realize that any resulting pain has a clear and definitive end AND I will see it all clearly, and do it without needing *anyone else* to fill any void is just 🥹🔥 I needed this. I understand conventional wisdom now

It went from "i *need* to be with someone" to "I am so whole on my own. Either add to my life and match the standards that I know I personally deliver on too or GTFO." Bc otherwise being single >>>> being partnered and I'm never compromising on values or standards EVER again

By the way it's not like I was *trying to* rebound but when attention came to me with appeal i didn't take time to slow down and evaluate my current state, what I truly needed, where I was at, and if the person was *really* for me. Now I do
Or like, I knew I should be single for longer but I was like "yolo let's just escape the pain bc we can" but it rly just extends it

Why scrap together a reimagined vision of a deity with amateur painting skills when there's so much "perfect art" that exists or that can be generated instantly with AI? It's not the same. The personal connection to capturing essence unfolds with each line. It's about the process

It's like flowy lines of curling hair paint themselves and a changing facial expression with each correction and re-correction has me studying and tweaking a visual persona that much more. You give paper a personality in a meticulous process
It's not about making "pretty art" or "technically sound" art, but art that you can feel a connection to in the process

It oddly helps me relate to the essence of a figure more as I meticulously try to capture her visually with detail, color, line, texture but also "make it mine" instead of what I see that's created by someone else

All good things take not only effort, but time too. One isn't a synonym for the other. When you take accountability for yourself at a certain point it's not a skill issue anymore so stop talking to yourself like it is; some processes straight up take time

This goes for injury, grief, release of resentment, building a skill. Yes, you can rest and take care of a wound. Yes, you can meditate. Yes, you can get your reps in. All that might reduce the time it takes to get to where you want to be, but it still needs time

There are a million meandering paths to get to the same destination & your appraisal of a subjective experience can't possibly be objective. Eg. psychedelics helping someone on the path of spiritual realizations: This isn't necessarily invalid/it can be valid. How would you know?

I understand the value of sober practice but to discount other ppl's experiences is narrow-minded.

That one movie you thought was stupid comedy could have changed another person's life and relationships.   

Is there value in insisting that your path and your taste is *the* way?

There's irony in believing you are dharmic while judging, prescribing, and defining the paths that others take. People realize and contextualize things exactly how they realize and contextualize them.
Is anyone saying onlyyy psychedelics and no practice and now you're a bodhisattva? Nope. But to discount them as valid incubators and accelerators for some is silly

You don't know another person's starting baseline or how they process new information. Yes, ppl get overconfident. But they also learn, refine, correct, grow 🤷🏽‍♀️ learn by doing, experiencing, curiosities

You can't possibly be tripping for life (physically impossible) so eventually the most intentional find their sustainable sober path of the pursuit of self-realization and liberation. But who is anyone to say what is valid/invalid inspiration for it?

Sometimes, sharing some of my thoughts feels like a double-edged sword. It can be helpful to earnest people but I can see how it's harmful when grafted for marketing and manipulation by people that are fundamentally self/power-focused and some seekers won't know the difference

Eg. where power, influence, and charisma come from. Would I love to speak more on it? For those just looking to better themselves and find their voice and empowerment, yes! But the exact same information can be used and even copied by ppl looking to control others/sell a persona

On principle, I know I should not seek to manage how ppl respond to info - that's their role. But also, I understand why some secrets are secrets & can & should be self-discovered, lest they get co-opted by people taking "shortcuts" for power over others vs. benevolent strength

Unless if this is discovered on your own or in small settings, the public sharing of some wisdom does have its pitfalls of misappropriation, misuse, and danger

Some ppl dunk on Instagram usage but here's how I use it for plans & making friends!

1) Posting stories as a way to share your hobbies & watch other peoples' to learn theirs. Reply with how you snowboard/draw/paint/like this artist too/etc & invite them to related events!

2) Following your favorite artists, comedians, festival official accounts etc and seeing which other friends are following that account as well - these are potential friends you can go to that event with. @katrinadlc and I saw Anna Akana bc I saw she also followed!

3) You can also see who liked certain posts for events coming up (will show underneath) - same logic as #2. "Hey I saw you liked this post too! Are you going? I was looking to go with someone"

4) Stories in general= conversation starters. Relate to something, say something :)

All the above require initiative. If you think it's too weird or too forward, imagine you get a message like that from someone else! You probably wouldn't feel bad thinking that someone else thought you'd be cool to hang with (you might feel the opposite of bad, in fact)

This stuff is second-nature to me now and I do it frequently so I assume it's obvious to people, but apparently it's not ahah 

Wanted to share in case it helps anyone :)

Oh I forgot!

5) You have mutual followers when you add someone = instant convo starter. "How do you know ___?" If it's someone I like, I'll even make a plan w all three of us sometimes. Can take the pressure off a 1x1 hang w someone you just met too (if you experience that)
